item.title: Sweden wanted fewer immigrants. It’s working and key industries could be feeling it.
item.dc:creator: Alejandro Lozada Cortés
item.content:encoded:
Sweden depends on foreign labor. In 2024, around one in every four employed persons was foreign-born. The rate was one in five for occupations requiring advanced education, and one in two for roles like caregivers, cleaners, harvesters and the likes. Although these figures include EU workers, according to official statistics around 75% of the working-age immigrants were born outside the block.

Even migration minister Johan Forssell, who leads Sweden’s immigration crackdown, recognizes that the country needs immigrants as much as it suspects them. Last year, Forssell argued for nine measures “to position Sweden as a top destination for talent”. Fast-tracked work permits, digital passport controls and a stronger “Brand Sweden” were some of the carrots in the government’s case to foreign workers.

In the past three years, however, the country has repeatedly chosen the stick. The salary threshold for work permits rose from 13 thousand kronor to 28 thousand, just to be increased again to 33 thousand. The government also championed the biggest overhaul in a generation for naturalization rules: minimum residence times increased from 5 to 8 years, and around 100 thousand people were left without any transition rules.

Data compiled and processed by The Local gives a first comprehensive look at the aggregated result of the government’s strategy. The evidence suggests Sweden could be becoming less attractive to foreign workers. All of our data and code is open source and can be found in this public repository. Find more details about our methodology at the end of this article.

For a second year in a row, Sweden has received less first-time work permit applications compared to 2015. Last year alone, the drop was 10%. In simple terms: the number of workers aspiring to get a work permit for the first time was lower in 2025 than a decade ago.

During the same period of time, rejection rates rose from 26% to 42%, marking a historic high. Both trends, the rapid decline in applications and the steep increase in rejections, began in 2022. Late that year, Ulf Kristersson of the Moderate Party took office as Prime Minister.

Together, these two forces have led to a significant drop in immigrant workers joining Sweden’s workforce. Key sectors like healthcare, software, agriculture, and education have all reached 10-year minima.

IT and Software, for example, had relatively stable levels of workers joining each year until 2022. Since then, it has only gone down. The latest figures show a 25% drop compared to ten years ago —steeper than the 14% reduction seen during the pandemic, which is also the last time numbers dropped.

In absolute numbers, it means that the sector went from around 3300 new workers in 2015 to 2500 in 2025. A steep reversal in a trend that had the country receiving around 4600 new tech workers every year for the majority of the last decade, excluding the height of the pandemic.

The story is almost the same for roles as different as education professionals, fishermen, and nurses. Agriculture, forestry, and fishery went from 4300 new workers ten years ago to 2400 last year; education, which includes primary teachers as well as university professors, from 263 to 99; and healthcare from 330 to 301. All historic lows.

Amanda Herzog’s business used to entail promoting Sweden as a destination for foreign workers. Six months ago, she was faced with a choice: “the business was successful, but the environment was getting worse”, she told The Local. “I couldn’t continue, in good faith, encouraging people to come to Sweden when I no longer believed it was safe for international professionals, especially if you needed a visa”.

Herzog noticed a change in trends when it came to immigrant labor. “A lot of companies started adding disclaimers everywhere, warning that they were unable to sponsor visas. IKEA was unable to sponsor a visa, are you kidding me? They have experience, resources, and immigration consultants on retainer. But they just started to drop like flies”.

Her experience is consistent with the data. Recruitment times in sectors that heavily rely on immigrant workers have multiplied as permits dropped, hinting that employers could be feeling the missing supply.

In the energy sector, there’s a clear step change in recruiting times around 2022. Ten years ago, it took recruiters 1.3 months to find a worker; by 2024, the latest available datapoint, that figure had doubled to 2.6 months. Manufacturing tells a similar story, going from 1.2 to 2.3 months —as does the technical and scientific sector, which saw a comparable increase over the same period.

The data for the total of the economy, however, shows a rather stationary pattern. This could be because the impact in recruiting times is focalized to specific sectors, but also because the measurement excludes agriculture, forestry and fishery workers. This section of the economy had one of the biggest drops in foreign labor supply.

“I no longer endorse Sweden as a good environment for international professionals”, concludes Herzog, who early this year decided to return to the United States after 8 years in Sweden. “It is living off of the old PR, but it’s no longer that country”.

With parliament elections coming in September, the country will face a choice: double their bets and deepen the anti-immigration escalation, or reverse course. Only time will tell if Swedes get the results they were promised, or a rude awakening. Numbers, eventually, will let us know.

Methodological notes

  1. We start the comparison in 2015. Older figures exist, but we found older data to be either completely missing for certain crucial calculations or incomplete enough to make it potentially misleading. Earlier years also use a significantly different reporting schema. Stretching the comparison further back would have added noise rather than insight. 2015 is the earliest year from which the occupational data is consistent and complete enough in our opinion.

  1. We grouped Migrationsverket’s occupations into broader categories. The migration office publishes work-permit figures against a very fine-grained occupational classification. At that level, numbers are too atomic to be statistically significant or reveal patterns. We added an intermediate layer of our own to remediate this: 21 broader occupation categories that sit between Migrationsverket’s granular roles and the headline totals.

This also solves a comparability problem: midway through the period Migrationsverket switched its underlying classification system (from SSYK96 to SSYK 2012), so the same job can appear under different labels in different years. Our intermediate classification maps both systems to the same categories, making the 2015 to 2025 comparison possible. Find the full mapping in our code.

  1. Recruitment times stop at the first quarter of 2024. The figures on how long recruitment takes come from Statistics Sweden (SCB). The first quarter of 2024 is simply the most recent data point SCB has published, so that is where this part of the analysis ends.

  1. For a full overview of our methodology, data and code, head to our public repository.

item.title: “We are free, son. They are bombing Caracas” —Latin America reckons with conflicting realities after U.S. attacks
item.description: Alberto Barrios and Jesús Baldonedo haven’t been able to get a proper night of sleep in years.
item.dc:creator: Alejandro Lozada Cortés
item.content:encoded:
Alberto Barrios and Jesús Baldonedo haven’t been able to get a proper night of sleep in years. Both men have a lot in common: they left Venezuela around 2016, fleeing hardship; they both ended up in Sweden five years ago; and they both glued their eyes to their phones as the images of U.S. aircraft bombing their homeland spread across the world.

“I haven’t been able to sleep properly since I left Venezuela. I seldom manage to. Tonight I will”, Alberto told The Local. For him, the U.S. attacks, which spanned three Venezuelan states and resulted in the capture of Nicolás Maduro, were the culmination of a decade-long dream: “I started crying, I was on my knees thanking God”.

For Jesús, on the contrary, the same explosions meant only devastation and ransacking of resources: “The United States killed Venezuelans this morning for one thing only, and that’s [our] oil [...]. It was cowardly to drop missiles on our territory while everyone was sleeping”. Jesús even joined demonstrators on Saturday afternoon as they gathered at Odenplan, Stockholm, to protest the U.S. bombings as well as the Israeli occupation of Gaza.

The demonstration, organized with the support, amongst others, of Swedish politician and member of Riksdag, Lorena Delgado Varas, called for people to “resist U.S. imperialism”. “The Swedish government must condemn the kidnapping of Venezuela’s president”, said Delgado in a post on Instagram.

The tale of these two men echoes a similar divide in Latin America, where governments and citizens alike are trying to understand the implications of the attacks on their future.

The governments of Colombia, Brazil, Mexico, and Chile, all left-leaning despite their differences, were quick to condemn the military incursion and express concern about its potential consequences. “[It] recalls the worst moments of interference in Latin American and Caribbean politics”, wrote on X the Brazilian president Inácio Lula da Silva.

“Today it is Venezuela, with the excuse of narco-terrorism and the declared intention of controlling its natural resources. Tomorrow it could be any other, with a different excuse”, wrote Gabriel Boric, president of Chile.

In stark contrast, Javier Milei, Argentina’s president, celebrated: “Venezuela is free. The Left cries. Freedom moves forward”. So did Antonio Kast, president-elect of Chile, and María Corina Machado, the best-known figure in Venezuelan political opposition. “We have fought for years, and it’s been worth it. What needed to happen is happening right now”, said Machado in an official statement.

Latin America had not experienced a forced regime change since 1989, when the U.S. ousted Panama’s military dictator Manuel Noriega, who had originally risen to power with its support.

Sandra Borda, who has taken part in shaping Colombia’s foreign policy and is a professor of international relations at Universidad de los Andes in Bogotá, Colombia, told The Local that “one should avoid the temptation of choosing between the hope of ousting Maduro and the need to condemn the attacks, without considering the possible consequences of the armed intervention”.

In Borda’s view, it’s increasingly clear that Maduro being out of power didn’t translate to regime change. “The U.S. has an incentive to keep a puppet government, threatened, instead of going into the complex and highly unpredictable business of democracy-building, especially if violence follows”.

For Tomás Molina, a Colombian philosopher and also a professor at Los Andes, “the new hemispheric doctrine of the U.S. reduces our room to maneuver, [...] and claims the right to use force against any group it labels a ‘cartel’. This means that the United States can bomb anyone it perceives as a threat to its interests”.

Molina argued publicly that a return to sovereignty in Venezuela is highly unlikely, insofar as all decisions regarding the country, its resources and institutions, will have to align with the interests of the northern superpower. “Some will be happy about this; those of us who believe in democracy will not”.

“We are free, son” —Alberto recalls hearing on the other side of the phone, as he was polishing the floors of his house, south of Stockholm— “They are bombing Caracas as we speak”.

“They were bombing Caracas. People died”, says Jesús. “I woke up with this eerie feeling, and then I saw on the phone they were bombing our country”.

For all the distance that today seems to stand between these two similar men, and the hopes and histories that they appear to represent, there’s a crucial intersection: a mutual recognition of the need for one another.

“There’s a great effort to be made by both sides if we are to reconcile amongst Venezuelans, for we have been filled with hatred”, says Jesús. “This is my message: peace, dialogue, and reconciliation”.

Subscribe to OMP for free to receive new posts and support my work.
